old_claim.note: Advanced Materials Science is a plausible dependency for a forecast technology and should be treated as speculative.

new_claim.note: The breakthrough came from solid-state materials work on oxide/perovskite structures, making advanced materials science an enabling research capability rather than a hard component prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Nobel lecture Part 1 describes the search from niobium alloys to layered copper-containing perovskite oxides and prior work on perovskite structural problems.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The high-Tc breakthrough was rooted in solid-state oxide/perovskite materials research.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports materials science as enabling research capability, not a hard component dependency.

ontology_before: The graph treated materials science as a weak speculative edge for a future forecast.
ontology_after: The graph treats materials science as an enabling capability for the established 1986 breakthrough.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to a pure theoretical-superconductivity concept rather than materials.
A more specific perovskite oxide materials node is added and this edge is rewired.
short_reason: High-Tc superconductors emerged from solid-state oxide materials work.
